retrieved_docs.title: Macrocephaly -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Sotos syndrome, also known as cerebral gigantism, is another cause of megalencephaly. Other features include frontal bossing, a high forehead, high arched palate, intellectual disability or developmental delay, hypertelorism, and a prominent jaw or pointed chin. Most cases of Sotos syndrome result through sporadic mutations in the NSD1 gene.

retrieved_docs.title: Macrocephaly -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: If present, an underlying cause or associated syndrome is important to be identified. However, benign familial macrocephaly as an isolated entity does not require significant workup and caregivers can be reassured.

retrieved_docs.title: Macrocephaly -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Benign familial macrocephaly is not associated with any complications. However, macrocephaly due to hydrocephalus can have serious complications, including death, if not promptly treated. Other complications associated with macrocephaly are seizures, developmental disabilities, and neurologic deficits.

retrieved_docs.title: Macrocephaly -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Cowden syndrome is another syndrome that gives the patient susceptibility to certain cancers. Patients with Cowden syndrome can have macrocephaly and can develop thyroid or breast cancers. Cowden syndrome is caused by mutations in the PTEN gene and is also autosomal dominant.

retrieved_docs.title: Macrocephaly -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis of macrocephaly is determined by the underlying cause.

retrieved_docs.title: Macrocephaly -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Depending on the findings on history and physical, referrals to developmental therapies, pediatric neurology, genetics, or neurosurgery may be warranted. Good communication during any of these transfers of care is crucial for the continuity of care of the patient.

retrieved_docs.title: Macrocephaly -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Click here for a simplified version. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Macrocephaly -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The other causes of anatomic megalencephaly are varied and often genetic. [2] Neurocutaneous disorders such as tuberous sclerosis, neurofibromatosis, and hypomelanosis of Ito may be associated with megalencephaly. Patients with autism spectrum disorder may have macrocephaly/megalencephaly. Achondroplasia may also present with megalencephaly or hydrocephalus.

retrieved_docs.title: Macrocephaly -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: To determine if the macrocephaly is related to another process or syndrome, a careful history should be taken with attention to birth measurements and growth trajectory, any other associated medical problems, a developmental history, and any family history of macrocephaly. For instance, it would be important to know if the patient has had any history of CNS trauma or infection. If there were meningitis or intraventricular hemorrhage in the neonatal state, hydrocephalus could develop as a result, and these patients should be followed closely. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Macrocephaly -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Fragile X syndrome's features are macrocephaly, intellectual disability, prominent ears and jaw, and macroorchidism. These features may not be as noticeable prior to puberty. Fragile X syndrome is caused most frequently by a CGG trinucleotide repeat in the FMR1 gene, which is located on the X chromosome.

retrieved_docs.title: Macrocephaly -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: When presented with an infant with otherwise normal growth and development and a normal neurologic exam, macrocephaly by itself is often a benign entity, and neurodevelopmental outcomes are good. [11] [12] In these cases, enlargement of the subarachnoid space may be seen on imaging. This may still be present in long-term follow-up, but no intervention needs to be made. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Central Nervous System, Malformations -- Clinical Significance -- Megalencephaly
retrieved_docs.content: Megalencephaly is an increased head size above two standard deviations. Clinically, it is more applicable to define it as brain size greater than three standard deviations above the mean to exclude familial megalencephaly. It occurs due to congenital defects in neuronal migration or abnormal cell proliferation, or a combination of both. Megalencephaly is classified based on etiology, genetic abnormalities in metabolism, and development. Mutations in genes controlling major molecular pathways like the phosphatidylinositol 3-kinase (PI3K/AKT) have been implicated. On the other hand, megalencephaly requires differentiation from macrocephaly, which is an unusual increase in occipitofrontal circumference (OFC) at least two standard deviations caused by structural abnormalities of the cranium, brain, or cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) and related structures. [21] [22] [5] [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Macrocephaly -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Megalencephaly may be anatomic, which refers to an increase in the size or number of cells in the brain without metabolic disease process, or maybe metabolic. Anatomic megalencephaly is most often familial and benign. In these cases, the patient is often born with a large head and a normal-sized body with a further rapid increase in head size over the first six months of life (head circumference increases by 0.6 to 1 cm rather than average 0.4 cm/week). Familial megalencephaly usually produces mild macrocephaly, typically 2 to 4 cms above the 90th percentile, and the growth curve follows above but parallel to 98th percentile. Family members may also have large heads. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Macrocephaly -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: If the patient is developmentally appropriate for the age and has a normal neurologic exam, there are no abnormal features to suggest a specific genetic syndrome, and there is no family history of any developmental or neurologic problems, then Weaver curves may be utilized using both parents' head circumferences. If the patient is within normal range after plotting on this curve, then no further evaluation is needed. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Macrocephaly -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Macrocephaly is described as a head circumference more than two standard deviations above the mean for gestational age and sex, which falls above the 97th percentile. Although erroneously used interchangeably with macrocephaly, megalencephaly is a distinct term to suggest increased growth of cerebral structure. Macrocephaly is a broader term to encompass megalencephaly and other causes of increased head size without cerebral overgrowths, such as subdural fluid collection.

retrieved_docs.title: Macrocephaly -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: It is important to understand that there are different etiologies of macrocephaly. This understanding is needed to differentiate benign macrocephaly from conditions that require acute investigation and intervention to prevent long term neurological deficits and developmental delays.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2680
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are recognized as a related group of adverse perinatal outcomes and are classified together as a distinct clinical category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1843
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation are classified under the term تقييد النمو الجنيني, which denotes fetal growth restriction. This condition is understood to be part of a broader, unspecified disorder that originates in the perinatal period, referred to as حالة غير محددة نشأت في الفترة المحيطة بالولادة. Recognizing that تقييد النمو الجنيني falls within this larger perinatal context helps clinicians assess and manage infants presenting with these growth‑related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2900
retrieved_docs.content: CONDITIONS OF FETAL GROWTH AND MALNUTRITION represent a collective group of perinatal problems that encompass short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation. Short gestation, defined as delivery before full term, often results in low birth weight because the infant has had insufficient time for normal growth. Low birth weight, in turn, frequently reflects fetal growth retardation, a condition in which the fetus does not achieve its genetically expected size. These three entities are closely interrelated, each contributing to the broader spectrum of fetal growth and malnutrition disorders, and together they increase the risk of adverse neonatal outcomes. Effective maternal care that optimizes nutrition and prevents premature delivery is essential for reducing the incidence of these interconnected conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2106
retrieved_docs.content: فالج is a general medical condition, and فالج طفلي represents a more specific, narrower form of فالج, indicating the infantile variant of this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 151
retrieved_docs.content: (unspecified condition originating in the perinatal period), situating fetal growth restriction within a larger, undefined perinatal condition category. Together, these relationships illustrate how gestational duration, low birth weight, fetal growth restriction, and associated perinatal disorders are organized within medical classification systems, aiding clinicians in diagnosing and managing newborns with short gestation and related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 2270
retrieved_docs.content: The clinical presentation described as “Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation” is used to define and classify that same set of perinatal abnormalities, and it is categorized under the term “تقييد النمو الجنيني,” which translates to fetal growth restriction. This grouping emphasizes that short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are collectively recognized as a single entity of fetal growth restriction in medical classification.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2149
retrieved_docs.content: Fetal disproportion due to fetal myelomeningocele is a prenatal growth abnormality that manifests during the fetal period. In the same developmental window, other fluid‑related fetal conditions also arise, including wanverhouding with foetale ascites, wanverhouding with foetale hydrops, and استسقاء دماغي للجنين مسبب لعدم التناسب (fetal cerebral hydrocephalus causing disproportion). These entities are all linked by their occurrence in the fetal period, reflecting a spectrum of fetal fluid accumulation and structural imbalance that can be identified through prenatal imaging and require coordinated obstetric assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1498
retrieved_docs.content: CONDITIONS OF FETAL GROWTH AND MALNUTRITION encompass the specific subgroup identified as short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation. This subgroup is both defined by and classified under the term تقييد النمو الجنيني (fetal growth restriction). تقييد النمو الجنيني, in turn, is linked to a broader, unspecified condition that arises in the perinatal period, described as حالة غير محددة نشأت في الفترة المحيطة بالولادة. Together, these relationships illustrate that short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are recognized components of fetal growth restriction, which itself forms part of a larger, not‑yet‑specified perinatal disorder.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 2443
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are collectively classified as an extremely premature birth of unspecified weight.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 460
retrieved_docs.content: organs may manifest as uterine cyst (كيسة رحمية) and vaginal bleeding, highlighting diagnostic considerations. Short stature (قصر القامة) is clinically associated with neonatal death, indicating that individuals with short stature require careful perinatal monitoring and intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 4 -- Community 2873
retrieved_docs.content: The condition referred to as اضطراب جنيني (fetal disorder) is classified as a type of مضاعفات الحمل (pregnancy complication). Recognizing that fetal disorders are encompassed within the broader category of pregnancy complications helps clinicians identify, monitor, and manage these issues during prenatal care, ensuring appropriate assessment and intervention for both maternal and fetal health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 4 -- Community 2811
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are collectively classified as تقييد النمو الجنيني, a condition denoting fetal growth restriction. This classification highlights the interrelated nature of these perinatal indicators, which together signal impaired intrauterine growth and may necessitate focused obstetric monitoring and intervention to improve neonatal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 102
retrieved_docs.content: and cardiovascular complications are a subset of the overall perinatal condition spectrum. Collectively, these interrelated entities illustrate how gestational age, birthweight, and fetal growth trajectories define a network of classifications—from specific gestational milestones (29‑30 weeks) to broad perinatal disorder groups—providing a comprehensive framework for understanding, diagnosing, and managing health issues in liveborn infants.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 2205
retrieved_docs.content: Fetal disproportion due to fetal myelomeningocele is a condition that manifests during the fetal period. The fetal period itself is linked to other prenatal abnormalities, including wanverhouding; foetale ascites and استسقاء دماغي للجنين مسبب لعدم التناسب (fetal hydrocephalus causing disproportion). Recognizing that fetal disproportion associated with myelomeningocele, as well as ascites and hydrocephalus, all occur within the same developmental window underscores the importance of comprehensive fetal assessment to identify and manage multiple concurrent anomalies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2901
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation is recognized as a specific perinatal condition that is classified as تقييد النمو الجنيني, indicating fetal growth restriction, and also as ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن}, denoting a birth at the highest degree of prematurity with an unspecified weight. This dual classification underscores the clinical importance of identifying both the limited fetal growth and the extreme prematurity associated with these presentations for appropriate medical management and follow‑up.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 4 -- Community 2818
retrieved_docs.content: Conditions of fetal growth and malnutrition are regarded as a parent category that includes the specific outcomes of short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation. The grouping of short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation serves as a classification label for these adverse perinatal conditions, emphasizing their interrelated nature in the context of impaired fetal development and inadequate nutrition.
